coredata.dc:title: An impedance sensor based on chitosan-carbon quantum dots for the detection sialic acid in humuan serum
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Zhihong
Yang, Xiaoling
Hua, Yingchen
Li, Ziquan
Liu, Yi
Lin, Yuehua
coredata.dc:description: Here, we introduce an ultra-sensitive electrochemical impedance sensor for the detection of sialic acid (SA) in human serum. We synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a simple hydrothermal method. By dispersing CQDs in chitosan (CS), a homogeneous CS-CQDs composite material can be obtained. CS-CQDs composite material has good biocompatibility and high stability, and can form a stable film on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode (CS-CQDs/GCE). In addition, based on the principle that boronic acid groups specifically recognize sialic acid, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (ABA) is used as a specific recognition molecule for sialic acid to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the prepared modified electrode (ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE). The morphology of the CS-CQDs composite material was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). At the same time, the electrochemical behavior of CS-CQDs composite materials was discussed through electrochemical technology. Studies have shown that the linear response range of the prepared ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE to SA is 0.02 ~ 3 mM. The detection limit is 5 μM (S/N = 3). The proposed ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE has high sensitivity, good repeatability and strong selectivity, and has achieved good results in the detection of real samples (human serum samples). Therefore, a practical detection platform for rapid, simple, selective and reliable detection of SA is provided.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Sialic acid
Impedance sensor
Chitosan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs and CS-CQDs composite
Characterization of the prepared CS-CQDs composite
Effect of incubation time in ABA solution
ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE measures the linear range and detection limit of SA
Reproducibility, selectivity and stability of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE
Application of this method in human serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangzhou Yuexiu District Science and Technology Plan Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Project (2016A010103039), Zhongshan Science and technology project (2019SYF7), Guangzhou Yuexiu District Science and Technology Plan Project (2017-WS-016)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM images of CS-CQDs composite (10 μm) and (200 nm) (B).

(A) The nyquist diagrams of EIS and (B) CVs of bare/GCE, CS-CQDs/GCE, ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE and upon addition of SA solutions (1 mM). CV parameters: Potential range is between −0.2 to + 0.6 V; Scan rate is 100 mV s−1. EIS parameters: 0.1 M, pH 7.8, PBS containing K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (5 mM, 1:1) and 0.1 M KCl; 60 frequencies within 10 kHz-1 Hz interval; amplitude 0.005 V; initial potential 0.22 V.
(A) EIS diagram of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE at different SA concentrations. (B) The SA concentration calibration curves of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE.
Preparation process of ABA/CS-CQDs composite material modified electrode.
Comparison of analytical parameters of SA determination at the ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE with other modified electrodes.
Sialic acid determination human serum sample by ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE (n = 6).

head.ce:title: An impedance sensor based on chitosan-carbon quantum dots for the detection sialic acid in humuan serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangdong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangdong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical And Chemical Engineering, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical And Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical And Chemical Engineering, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangdong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we introduce an ultra-sensitive electrochemical impedance sensor for the detection of sialic acid (SA) in human serum. We synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a simple hydrothermal method. By dispersing CQDs in chitosan (CS), a homogeneous CS-CQDs composite material can be obtained. CS-CQDs composite material has good biocompatibility and high stability, and can form a stable film on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode (CS-CQDs/GCE). In addition, based on the principle that boronic acid groups specifically recognize sialic acid, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (ABA) is used as a specific recognition molecule for sialic acid to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the prepared modified electrode (ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE). The morphology of the CS-CQDs composite material was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). At the same time, the electrochemical behavior of CS-CQDs composite materials was discussed through electrochemical technology. Studies have shown that the linear response range of the prepared ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE to SA is 0.02 ~ 3 mM. The detection limit is 5 μM (S/N = 3). The proposed ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE has high sensitivity, good repeatability and strong selectivity, and has achieved good results in the detection of real samples (human serum samples). Therefore, a practical detection platform for rapid, simple, selective and reliable detection of SA is provided.

ce:section.ce:para: Sialic acid (SA) is a monosaccharide with nine carbon atoms, which is usually located at the non-reducing ends of glycoproteins and glycolipids on the cell surface [1-3]. SA plays an important role in recognizing signals in the brain and regulating the life cycle, so it has attracted extensive attention [4-6]. Recent studies have shown that SA is a biomarker of some cancers and other diseases [7-11], such as brain tumors, cardiovascular diseases, leukemia, lung cancer and other related diseases [12-14]. When normal cells undergo pathological changes, glycolipids and glycoproteins on the cell membrane are synthesized in large quantities and abnormally transformed, resulting in the over-expression of SA residues in the serum samples of cancer patients on the cell surface, which promotes the increase of SA concentration [15]. The detection of SA levels in serum samples has important clinical reference value for tumor screening and diagnosis of potential diseases. Since the treatment of life-threatening cancers directly depends on the ability to detect certain biomarkers in body fluids or tissues at an early stage, effective diagnosis of the early or asymptomatic stage of the disease can buy time for its ultra-early intervention, thereby increase the effectiveness of treatment and reduce the incidence of disease [16].

In order to qualitatively and quantitatively analyze SA in biological samples, some analytical methods for SA detection have been reported, such as chromatography [17,18], fluorescence method [19], colorimetry [20], quartz crystal Microbalance (QCM) [21], enzymatic method [22], potentiometer method [23], enhanced Raman scattering [24], local surface plasmon resonance [25] and electrochemical biosensor [19,26]. Each assay has its advantages and disadvantages. For instance, chromatographic method requires complicated pretreatment when detecting actual samples, which is time-consuming and costly. Although the enzymatic method has high sensitivity, its stability is poor. The colorimetric method and the fluorescence method are susceptible to interference during detection, and the sensitivity is low. Electrochemical technology is widely used in SA detection because of its relatively simple operation, sensitivity and high selectivity [23,27,28]. Therefore, the development of low-cost, simple, fast and sensitive non-enzymatic electrochemical methods for SA detection is still of great significance.

It is reported that the interaction between boric acid and diol-containing compounds is through reversible ester bonds. Therefore, boronic acid can bind to the triol side chain of SA and act as a recognition molecule for SA detection [29-31].

Studies have shown that due to the agglomeration tendency of nanoparticles, there are many limitations in the use of nanoparticles, which shows that it is important to control the dispersion of substances [32]. The linear chain of chitosan (CS) contains reactive amino groups and hydroxyl groups. It has excellent film-forming ability, good adhesion, non-toxic, cheap and easy to chemical modification. Thus, CS has gradually been used to construct sensors [33-35]. CS is an ideal choice for dispersing nanomaterials and manufacturing nanocomposites. In addition, CS can be used as a dispersant for nanomaterials to form a stable composite material, and can be fixed on the electrode surface to form a stable film [36].

Nowadays, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials that have been extensively studied. CQDs is a quasi-spherical nanoparticle with a large number of polar functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl), which has the advantages of good water solubility, high light stability and excellent biocompatibility [37-39]. CQDs have a wide range of sources, low price, unique electron transfer ability and large specific surface area, which makes them have excellent electrochemical reaction performance [40].These unique properties make CQDs widely used in bioimaging [41], drug delivery [42], photocatalysis [43], electrocatalysis [44] and sensing [45]. CQDs have shown excellent applications in many fields, and the application of sensor detection has received extensive attention.

Recently, we used citric acid as a carbon source to synthesize CQDs through the classic hydrothermal method. CS and CQDs are combined through an amide reaction to form a CS-CQDs composite. In addition, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (ABA) is introduced as the recognition molecule of SA, so that the prepared modified electrode has a specific recognition effect on SA. In the current work, we had optimized several experimental parameters and studied the electrochemical performance of CS-CQDs composites. At the same time, the proposed modified electrode was also used to detect SA in human serum and urine samples.

3-aminophenylboronic acid (ABA), sialic acid (SA), glycine (Gly), glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys) and sucrose (Suc) were purchased from Nine-Dinn Chemistry (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. (China). Citric acid (CA), chitosan (CS), 1-ethyl-(3-dimethyl Aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS), Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). L-ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, (China). KCl was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (China). Ferric chloride hexahydrate potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6) and potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). A phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixing 0.1 M Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 stock solutions. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment. All of the chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade and required no further purification. Deionized water was used for the preparation of the samples in all experiments.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed using a NanoScope Multimode AFM (Veeco, USA) using the tapping mode AFM. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet iS5, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) spectroscopy was carried out to analyze the functional groups. The morphology of modified electrodes was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a LEO1430 vs. (Carl Zeiss, Germany) instrument.

The CHI660E electrochemical workstation was used to obtain cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data. The electrochemical experiments were carried out on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua instrument Co., LTD.) with a conventional three-electrode cell. Bare or modified GCE utilizes working electrodes; Ag/AgCl and platinum are used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out in 0.1 M, pH 7.8, PBS containing K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (5 mM, 1:1) and 0.1 M KCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and CS-CQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolve citric acid (5 g) in 40 mL pure water, then transfer this solution to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lining, put the lining into a stainless steel reactor and put it in an oven, and react at 150 ℃ for 150 min . After the reaction, the solution was taken out after the oven temperature dropped to room temperature, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was separated, and dialyzed with a 200 Da dialysis bag for 2 days. The water was changed every 5 h, and finally the CQDs were lyophilized.

0.4 g of CS powder in 40 mL of 1% acetic acid and place it in an ultrasonic bath for 2 h to obtain a CS solution. The 6 mg/mL CQDs solution and CS solution were mixed in the same volume ratio, and then sonicated for 30 min to prepare the CS-CQDs composite.

Before each experiment, GCE was polished with 0.05 μm alumina powder and rinsed thoroughly in ethanol and deionized water for 5 min. 2 μl of CS-CQDs composite was dropped on the surface of GCE and dried under infrared light irradiation to obtain CS-CQDs/GCE. In order to fix ABA on the electrode surface, the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CS-CQDs composite need to be activated. Therefore, CS-CQDs/GCE was activated in a solution containing 5 mM EDC and NHS at 37 °C for 30 min. Place the activated CS-CQDs/GCE in a 50 mM ABA solution and incubate at 37 °C for 30 min. Wash the electrode with deionized water to prepare ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE and place it for use. Each step of electrode modification was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The change in charge transfer resistance (Rct) observed in the absence and presence of sialic acid is used as the prepared modified electrode response to determine the concentration of sialic acid (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared CS-CQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The CS-CQDs composite plays a key role in the manufacture of electrochemical impedance sensors. Fig. 1 shows the SEM image of CS-CQDs. In Fig. 1A, it can be seen that CS-CQDs form a uniform thin layer on the electrode surface. Fig. 1B shows the unique spherical structure of CQDs, and it can also be observed that the average diameter of the CS-CQDs composite is about 200 nm. Due to the existence of CS, the dispersibility of CQDs is promoted, and a stable CS-CQDs composite is obtained [46].

The surface morphology changes of CS-CQDs composite materials were observed using AFM. Fig. S1 shows the AFM image of the CS-CQDs composite. The AFM image of the CS-CQDs composite proves that the dispersion of CQDs in CS is very good (Fig. S1A). It can be seen from Fig. S1B that the surface of CS-CQDs is rough with a roughness of about 154.5 nm.

The FTIR spectrum of the CS-CQDs composite is shown in Fig. S2. The peak at 1400 cm−1 on CS-CQDs originates from the stretching vibration of the O–H bond in CQDs; the peaks at 1700 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of –COOH in CQDs [47,48]. The peaks at 2800 and 2900 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of C–H in CS [49]; and the peaks at 1621 and 1578 cm−1 originate from –CONH-, indicating that CS and CQDs are combined through amide bonds [46,50].

EIS and CV are widely used to monitor the assembly process of sensors because of their sensitivity and instant response to electroactive substances. EIS is composed of a similar semicircular part produced by the electron transfer limited process at low frequencies and a linear part indicating the diffusion-limited electrochemical process at high frequencies [21]. In the EIS diagram, the diameter of the semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the modified layer, and the actual resistance value reflects its blocking behavior to the electrode. Zview software was used to fit the EIS data to obtain the corresponding Rct value.

The EIS (Fig. 2 A) and CV (Fig. 2B) methods were used to characterize each process of electrode modification. As can be seen from Fig. 2A, compared with bare/GCE, the semicircular diameters of CS-CQDs/GCE, ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE and SA/ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE gradually became larger, indicating that the impedance sensor was successfully prepared. The CV results on the interface characteristics of the above samples (Fig. 2B) were consistent with the EIS data. As the electrode was gradually modified, the resulting redox current value was gradually decreasing. CS binds to CQDs through strong hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions to form CS-CQDs composite. When the CS-CQDs composite material was fixed to the electrode surface, a thin film was formed on the electrode surface, which hinders the electron transfer process. When ABA and SA were fixed to the electrode, the hindering effect during the electron transfer process will increase significantly. Because ABA and SA are combined through a reversible ester bond, and SA is usually negatively charged at physiological pH, it will repel the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and hinder the electron transfer process [51].Fig. 3

In the detection of SA, the concentration of CQDs, the incubation time of ABA and the pH value of the solution directly affect the electrochemical signal, which is closely related to the electrochemical sensitivity. Therefore, these influencing factors were explored.

The abundant surface functional groups (–OH,–NH2, etc.) on CQDs can provide favorable anchoring and active sites for the preparation of multi-component and high-performance composite materials [40,52]. Therefore, it is necessary to study the influence of CQDs concentration on the results. In the case of 1 mM SA, the effect of CQDs concentration between 1 and 10 mg/mL was studied. It can be seen from Fig. S3(A-B) that when the concentration of CQDs is between 1 and 6 mg/mL, the Rct value keeps increasing, and the Rct value tends to be stable at 6 mg/mL. This is because CQDs can not only change the morphology and crystal phase, but also optimize the electronic structure, provide new active centers and defect sites, thereby improving stability [53]. The results show that when the concentration of CQDs is 6 mg/mL, CQDs can stably bind to CS and form a stable film on the electrode surface. Therefore, 6 mg/mL CQDs were selected for the following experiments.

Studies have shown that the electrochemical behavior of boric acid-based sensors is significantly affected by pH. Therefore, we investigated the influence of pH on the electrochemical behavior of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE. The pH change process from 6.2 to 8.0 was tested with 1 mM SA. As shown in Fig. S4(A-B), as the pH value of PBS increased, the Rct value continued to increase. The results showed that a larger Rct value can be obtained when pH = 7.8. Hence, we chose a pH value of 7.8 as the optimal condition for the next experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time in ABA solution
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of ABA as a recognition probe is the most important condition for the successful determination of SA. Therefore, the incubation time of the modified electrode in the ABA solution will be the key to obtain the best sensitivity for SA detection. In the presence of 1 mM SA, the effect of the ABA solution incubation time between 5 and 50 min was studied (Fig. S5 (A-B)). It can be seen from Fig. S5B that when the incubation time is between 5 and 30 min, the Rct value gradually increases. This is because the activated CS-CQDs/GCE can be combined with ABA through amide reaction. As the incubation time increased, ABA gradually fixed on the surface of the modified electrode, the Rct value changed significantly, and the maximum Rct value was obtained at 30 min. When the incubation time was 40 and 50 min, too much ABA on the modified electrode hindered the process of electron transfer, and the Rct value was significantly reduced. The results show that when the incubation time is 30 min, the ABA immobilized on the surface of the modified electrode has reached a saturated state. Therefore, an incubation time of 30 min was selected for the following studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE measures the linear range and detection limit of SA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the analytical performance of the proposed modified electrode, under optimal conditions, different concentrations of SA were measured and corresponding EIS diagrams and linear relationship diagrams were obtained. In Fig. 4A, as the concentration of SA increased, the Rct value in the EIS graph also increased significantly. When the SA concentration is within 0.02 ~ 3 mM, the Rct value has a linear relationship with log c, and the detection limit is 5 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4B). Compared with other sialic acid sensors, the proposed impedance sensor exhibits satisfactory performance (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, selectivity and stability of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Under the best experimental conditions, the repeatability was evaluated by using the prepared ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE to measure the 0.1 mM SA continuously five times. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 3.8%, showing a good reproducibility.

The selectivity of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE for SA detection to different interfering species was also evaluated. It can be observed in Fig. S6 that the comparison of the electrochemical response of SA with interfering substances such as cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly) , sucrose (Suc), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and glutamic acid (Glu). Among them, the concentrations of SA and interfering substances were both 0.1 mM. As shown in Fig. S6, at the same concentration, ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE had a certain Rct value for interfering substances, because substances containing glycol structure (such as sucrose) will form a reversible covalent bond with ABA, thereby interfering with sensor detection [64]. However, in the presence of interfering substances, ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE still showed the highest Rct value for SA. This is because compared with sugars, the binding constant of SA and boric acid is very high in physiological or acid buffer solutions [29]. The results showed that the presence of interfering substances hardly interferes with the determination of SA. Thus, the proposed ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE had acceptable selectivity for SA measurement.

In addition, we also tested the time stability of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE. Store the prepared ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE at 4 °C. One week later, the same ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE was used to detect the same concentration of SA. The ratio of the Rct recorded in the experiment on the 7th day compared with the Rct obtained in the previous experiment was 94.9%. The result showed that the proposed modified electrode has good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of this method in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: According to the reported detection method, the total level of SA in human serum is 1.6 to 2.9 mM [19]. The prepared ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE was used to determine SA in human serum samples to evaluate the applicability and application potential of the method. Human serum samples came from the First Affiliated Hospital of Guangdong Pharmaceutical University. Using the standard addition method, the healthy human serum samples were diluted 50 times, and then the experiment was carried out. The linear curve equation is used to calculate the experimental results, and the corresponding results are listed in Table 2 . These results indicate that the proposed modified electrode has broad prospects as a reliable technique for measuring SA in practical applications.

In summary, we have successfully designed a simple electrochemical impedance sensing strategy. This method uses 3-aminophenylboronic acid as the recognition molecule of sialic acid, which can quickly detect sialic acid in human serum. The linear range of ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE in SA detection is 0.02 ~ 3 mM, and the detection limit is 0.05 μM (S/N = 3). In addition, ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE also has the advantages of good repeatability, high stability, and strong selectivity. Using ABA/CS-CQDs/GCE to perform recovery experiments in human serum and urine samples, the recovery rate obtained is 96.5 ~ 105%, and the RSD is less than 5%. Therefore, the proposed modified electrode with fast EIS response and simple operation provides a new idea and platform for SA detection, and has broad prospects in the fields of biomedical diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Project (2016A010103039), Zhongshan Science and technology project (2019SYF7), Guangzhou Yuexiu District Science and Technology Plan Project (2017-WS-016)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106520.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
